ex01-001.text: Lock in 75: Stephen says seventy-five souls came down into Egypt.

ex01-002.text: Picture eleven brothers at the gate while Joseph is already inside Egypt. He is the one not named among those who 'came into' Egypt here.

ex01-003.text: New king, no Joseph: the verse says a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.

ex01-004.text: POR = Pithom, On, and Raamses. Goshen is the trap answer, not one of the store cities.

ex01-005.text: Pour the answer into POR: Pithom, On, Raamses.

ex01-007.text: Shiphrah ships them; Puah pulls them.

ex01-009.text: Feared God -> households. Tie the reward to the reason.

ex01-010.text: Pharaoh points at the river: every Hebrew son goes in. That one word is the answer.

ex02-002.text: BA beats GP: Bulrushes and asphalt, not gopher wood and pitch.

ex02-003.text: Bath becomes rescue: Pharaoh's daughter goes down to bathe and comes up with Moses.

ex02-005.text: Reared amid the pleasures of the court, Moses goes out and chooses to see his brethren's suffering rather than enjoy his own ease. This is the soul's first ascent: to lay down comfort and take up the affliction of God's people, esteeming reproach with them greater than the treasures of Egypt — prefiguring the greater Deliverer who, being rich, became poor for our sake.
ex02-005.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

ex02-005.text: Blank = suffering; Hebrews remembers Moses choosing affliction with God's people over Egypt's pleasures.

ex02-006.text: Left, right, no one; strike, bury, sand.

ex02-007.text: Moses flees to Midian and drops beside a well.

ex02-008.text: Bullies at the well, Moses to the rescue, seven sisters saved. True.

ex02-010.text: Gershom is the name tied to Moses saying he had been a stranger in a foreign land.

ex02-011.text: GERshom = 'a stranger there' — hear the 'GER,' the sojourner with no inheritance in the land. By his son's name Moses confesses that this world is not his resting place — a fitting name for the man who will lead a whole nation of sojourners.
Gershom means stranger there, tied to Moses saying he was a stranger in a foreign land.

ex02-012.text: Heard. Remembered. Saw. Knew. Rescue is starting.

ex03-001.text: HOReb = HOLY hello. Horeb is where God calls Moses from the burning bush.

ex03-002.text: Burning, not burnt: the Unburnt Bush is an Orthodox image of the Virgin who bore the divine Fire and was not consumed.
ex03-002.source: Orthodox Church in America, Unburnt Bush Icon of the Mother of God

ex03-003.text: Not cloak—sandals. Holy ground means shoes off.

ex03-004.text: Sandals off: Gregory reads Moses' ascent as shedding attachment before drawing near to God.
ex03-004.source: Orthodox Arts Journal, Divine Patterns in the Life of Moses

ex03-005.text: Hineni memory hook: Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah also answer God's call with 'Here I am.'

ex03-006.text: A-I-J: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. Jesus uses those three names to teach that God is not God of the dead but of the living.

ex03-007.text: Picture hills dripping with milk and golden honey—the exact opposite of Egypt's brick dust.

ex03-008.text: CHAP GHJ = Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites. No P—Philistines are not on the list.

ex03-009.text: Seven names, one will do: CHAP GHJ. Any one of those seven peoples is a right answer.

ex03-010.text: Moses asks, 'Who am I?' God answers, 'I will be with you.' The answer is not Moses' résumé but God's presence.

ex03-011.text: EXISTING ONE = the One who just IS. Snap on the opening words: I AM THE EXISTING ONE.

ex03-012.text: Turn the Name into a message: THE EXISTING ONE sent me to you.

ex04-001.text: Five fingers, five objections; finger five is 'send someone else,' and then the Lord's anger burns.

ex04-002.text: Three signs: snake, snow-hand, blood-water. Almond blossoms are the odd one out.

ex04-003.text: Pharaoh wore the rearing cobra as a royal emblem, so Moses' serpent-sign hits Egypt right on the crown.
ex04-003.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4; Metropolitan Museum of Art, Uraeus

ex04-004.text: Moses pulls out a snow-white hand, hides it again, and pulls it out normal.

ex04-005.text: River to blood is the preview of plague one, and Egypt even personified the Nile in the god Hapi.
ex04-005.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4 and 7; Encyclopaedia Britannica, Hapi

ex04-006.text: Slow of tongue. Tongue is the word you need.

ex04-007.text: Aaron = the mouth. Moses gets the message; Aaron says it out loud.

ex04-008.text: Firstborn is not just first in line; it is heir-status. So 'Israel is My firstborn son' marks Israel as God's heir-son.
ex04-008.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4; Ancient Faith Ministries, The Sacrifices of Righteousness

ex04-009.text: Chapter 4 flashes the ending early: free My firstborn, or your firstborn dies. Exodus 12 is where that warning lands at Passover.

ex04-010.text: Roadside shock scene: Zipporah grabs a flint, circumcises her son, and the danger stops.

ex04-011.text: Night camp memory hook: sudden threat, flint, blood, then the attack stops.

ex05-001.text: Freedom is not just escape; it is a feast. Moses says 'feast in the wilderness,' and Paul gives the click: Christ our Passover is sacrificed, so keep the feast.

ex05-002.text: Pharaoh asks, 'Who is the LORD?'—so God turns Egypt into the answer sheet. Exodus says Egypt will know the LORD, and God will execute judgment on Egypt's gods.

ex05-003.text: Pharaoh hears 'Let go' and slams on the gas: same bricks, no straw. The first answer to freedom was MORE work.

ex05-004.text: Three S's: Same bricks, Stripped straw, Sore backs. Pharaoh removed the straw but kept the score.

ex05-005.text: STRAW = Still The Required Amount, Worse. No straw handed out; same brick count shouted out.

ex05-006.text: Picture middle managers taking the beating: Egyptian whips above them, impossible brick quotas below them. Who got hit? The Israelite foremen.

ex05-007.text: Chapter 5 is the cliffhanger: Moses obeys, and things get worse before they get better. Dark brick-yard night comes before rescue dawn.

ex06-001.text: Lock the blank to the Name: 'I am the LORD.' For the bigger Bible hook, Jesus says, 'Before Abraham was, I AM.'

ex06-002.text: Seven 'I wills' march like a rescue countdown: bring out, rescue, redeem, take, be God, bring in, give. Pharaoh says 'No'; God says 'I will' seven times.

ex06-003.text: For Exodus 6, look for gifts TO Israel: out, rescue, redeem, take, be God, bring in, give. 'Destroy Egypt's gods' is the trap answer because it is not one of the seven 'I wills.'

ex06-004.text: REDEEM = rescue with a price tag. God says, 'I will redeem you,' and Peter says we are redeemed with Christ's precious blood.

ex06-005.text: Lock it as a two-part pledge: 'My people; your God.' Exodus says it in the rescue story, and Revelation echoes it when God dwells with His people.

ex06-006.text: Hope hit a wall: their breath was crushed by slave labor. Picture people so beaten down that even good news cannot get past the bruises.

ex06-007.text: R-S-L: Reuben, Simeon, Levi. The list sprints to Levi because Moses and Aaron are coming from that line—third name, target reached.

ex06-008.text: Levi's sons are G-K-M: Gershon, Kohath, Merari. Amram is Kohath's kid, so he is Levi's grandson—not Levi's son.

ex06-009.text: Levi → Kohath → Amram → Moses and Aaron. Kohath is the K in the middle of the family chain.

ex06-010.text: Amram = A-M-M dad: Aaron, Moses, Miriam. One father, three famous siblings.

ex06-011.text: JocheBED: put the baby-answer to bed—Moses' mother is Jochebed. Amram + Jochebed = the Moses house.

ex06-013.text: EliSHEba—watch the SHE: she is Aaron's wife. The question asks for the woman beside Aaron, not one of his sons.

ex06-015.text: Aaron's altar lineup has four names: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Count N-A-E-I: four priestly sons, not three.

ex06-016.text: Picture the four sons in line: the two elder, Nadab and Abihu, fall consumed for offering strange fire before the Lord. The priesthood carries on through the two who remain, Eleazar and Ithamar — both names opening with a vowel. The vowel-pair endures where the consonant-pair falls.
The vowel pair is E and I: Eleazar and Ithamar. Think 'E-I, E-I'—the two vowel-start names are the answer.

ex07-001.text: Hard heart, huge wonders. Pharaoh's heart becomes a stone stage, and God piles up signs and WONDERS on it—remember the blank is WONDERS.

ex07-002.text: Aaron Ate 3 more = A-8-3. Moses was 80, Aaron was 83: the older brother is the 83-year-old.

ex07-003.text: 80 = eight decades: picture Moses, 80 years old, standing straight in Pharaoh’s throne room. Pharaoh sees old; God sends bold.

ex07-004.text: Aaron drops the rod; it drops Pharaoh’s jaw — a stick turns into a serpent right on the throne-room floor.

ex07-005.text: The magicians copy the sign: rods down, snakes up. But copying a sign is not controlling it — Aaron’s serpent is still the boss.

ex07-006.text: Snake-eating-snake: Aaron’s serpent swallows the whole magic show. Remember it as: God’s rod eats Egypt’s rods.

ex07-007.text: Egypt trusted the Nile for life; Hapi personified its flooding and fertility. In plague one, the life-river bleeds — a sharp reminder that the Lord judges Egypt’s gods.
ex07-007.source: Encyclopaedia Britannica, Hapi; Bible Gateway, Exodus 12:12

ex07-008.text: Fresh-water checklist: river, streams, canals, ponds, reservoirs — even vessels. The sea is the odd one out: S = skipped.

ex07-010.text: Seven days, one whole week: the Nile stays red long enough for every day on the calendar to smell it. Remember: seven days of dread by the river of red.

ex07-011.text: Magicians can copy the red-water trick, so the answer is True. They make more blood-water, not rescue-water.

ex08-001.text: Heqet was the frog-shaped face of fertility; God turns that symbol into a hopping nightmare. Egypt’s “life” idol becomes impossible to live with.
ex08-001.source: Encyclopaedia Britannica, Heqet; Bible Gateway, Exodus 12:12

ex08-002.text: Moses gives the message; Aaron gives the motion. Aaron’s arm brings the frog swarm.

ex08-003.text: Frogs invaded Houses, Bedrooms, Ovens, Kneading bowls — your bed and your bread defiled, no clean corner left. The temples of the gods are NOT named as a refuge; the idols cannot even keep the frogs out of their own house.
Frogs crash the house, bedroom, oven, and dough bowl — bed and bread. “Temples” is the trick option; it is not in the frog invasion list.

ex08-005.text: The magicians make the frog problem worse: more frogs, not fewer. Egypt’s magic has a plus button, no minus button.

ex08-006.text: Pharaoh asks, Moses prays, frogs drop. Remember the order: beg, pray, dead frogs.

ex08-007.text: Relief comes in piles: dead frogs heaped up until Egypt smells like a rotten swamp. Heaps = hills of dead frogs.

ex08-008.text: Frogs gone, promise gone. Pharaoh’s “yes” evaporates the moment Egypt can breathe again.

ex08-009.text: Aaron strikes the dust and the very ground breeds biting gnats — the lowliest element rising against the proud. 'Dust to dust to bugs': the third plague is born from the earth itself, not from the sky.
Dust to dust to bugs: Aaron hits the ground, and the ground hits Egypt back with gnats.

ex08-010.text: Dust does what magicians cannot: it turns into living gnats. Genesis says God formed man from dust; Exodus says the dust-plague makes the magicians tap out: “the finger of God.”

ex08-012.text: “Finger of God” is the magicians’ tap-out phrase: their arts hit a wall at the gnats. The same phrase marks God’s Law on tablets and Christ’s power over demons, so these three words carry serious Bible weight.
ex08-012.source: Bible Gateway, Exodus 8:19; Exodus 31:18; Deuteronomy 9:10; Luke 11:20

ex08-015.text: Plague four draws a giant border line: Egypt buzzes, Goshen rests. Remember the fly plague as the first “no-fly zone” for Israel.

ex08-016.text: Pharaoh offers a leash, not liberty: worship here, or do not go far. By verse 32, the answer is still no — he hardens again.

ex09-001.text: Plague five hits Egypt's barn, and Scripture later names the bigger point: God was judging Egypt's gods. Remember it as a barnyard takedown—livestock falls, idols lose, Israel's herds live.

ex09-003.text: God draws a line before Passover ever arrives: Egypt's herds die, Israel's do not. Picture a divine boundary fence around Goshen.

ex09-004.text: Moses throws furnace ashes like gray confetti; when the dust lands, Egypt breaks out in boils. Ashes up, sores out.

ex09-005.text: Ashes up, boils pop: Moses throws furnace dust, and skin erupts. Up goes ash; out come boils.

ex09-006.text: Egypt's magicians copied early signs, but boils bench them. By plague six they cannot even stand before Moses.

ex09-008.text: F and B got flattened: Flax and Barley. When hail hits, remember the two battered crops by saying, 'Flax broken, barley broken.'

ex09-010.text: 'Late equals safe.' Wheat and spelt ripen later and had not yet grown up, so the hail passed them by. The unripened crops survived.

ex09-011.text: Some Egyptians actually listened. During the hail warning, fear ran indoors and pride stayed in the field.

ex09-013.text: Pharaoh says, 'I have sinned,' then sins again as soon as the hail stops. Mouth-confession, hard heart—that is why the statement is False.

ex10-002.text: East starts both Exodus wind scenes: east brings locusts here, and east later drives back the sea. For this question, think, 'East = insects enter.'

ex10-005.text: Pharaoh offers a men-only exit and tries to keep the families in Egypt. Men may go; everyone does not—so the answer is False.

ex10-007.text: Three dark days: count them on three fingers in a blackout. Egypt's lights stay out for THREE.

ex10-008.text: Egypt sat in blackout, but Israel had light at home. Remember the contrast as 'dark in Egypt, lamps in Goshen.'

ex10-010.text: Picture Moses checking the barn door: not one hoof—not even a toenail—stays in Egypt. Hoof means every animal goes.

ex11-001.text: After nine plagues, God says one more is left. The last blow is the death of the firstborn, so remember: darkness was not the end.

ex11-002.text: Set the clock to 12:00. At midnight the Lord goes through Egypt—deep night, exact hour, unforgettable word.

ex11-003.text: Do not picture Israel sprinting out in Exodus 11. Picture a giant PAUSE button: one more plague is announced, then Exodus 12 gives the Passover before the exit.

ex11-004.text: Picture the Israelites walking out with shiny exit tickets: SILVER and GOLD. Not empty-handed—jingling-handed.

ex12-001.text: No spots, no shortcuts: the lamb had to be “without blemish.” St. Peter later calls Christ a lamb “without blemish and without spot,” so the phrase sticks: spotless lamb, spotless Christ.

ex12-003.text: Ewe? Ew—no. The Passover lamb was MALE: boy lamb, one year old, no blemish.

ex12-004.text: TEN tags the lamb; FOURTEEN is kill day.

ex12-005.text: TEN gets the tag; FOURTEEN gets the fire. Kill day = 14.

ex12-006.text: Paint a red goalpost on the door: two side posts and the top lintel. No blood on the floor.

ex12-007.text: Top and sides, not the step: red goalpost, no red doormat. Doorposts and lintel—not threshold.

ex12-008.text: Fire, Flat, Bitter: fire-roasted lamb, flat unleavened bread, bitter herbs. Three bites, one night.

ex12-009.text: Do not boil the lamb—picture it on fire. Justin Martyr says the roasted Passover lamb was arranged in the form of a cross, so “roasted with fire” sticks: the fiery lamb points to the Cross.
ex12-009.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 40

ex12-010.text: Leaven is the little puff that takes over the whole lump. Paul connects Passover to Christ and says to keep the feast with the “unleavened bread” of sincerity and truth—so the answer is no yeast.

ex12-011.text: Bitter blanks bite: lamb, flat bread, and bitter HERBS. If your tongue winces, the answer is herbs.

ex12-012.text: PASSOVER is the feast name because God passed over the blood-marked house. The blank is PASSOVER.

ex12-013.text: At midnight the FIRSTBORN stamp goes dark across Egypt—palace, prison, even livestock. The answer is not “some”; it is every firstborn in Egypt.

ex12-014.text: Look for the red mark, not the address. God says that when He sees the blood, He will pass over—so the spared houses are the blood-covered houses.

ex12-015.text: Pharaoh’s palace was not plague-proof. Exodus names Pharaoh’s own firstborn in the midnight judgment—yes, his son too.

ex12-016.text: Four-three-oh, time to go. Canaan plus Egypt on the scoreboard: 430.

ex12-018.text: 600,000 is the men-only headline; families are still off-screen. Think: 600K men, plus women and children.

ex12-019.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Four days under inspection prove the lamb without blemish before it is slain; chosen on the 10th, killed on the 14th.
TEN gets the tag; FOURTEEN gets the fire. Set apart on 10, killed on 14.

ex13-001.text: Think of the firstborn as stamped with a Passover tag: "Mine." God spared Israel's firstborn, so the firstborn is consecrated back to Him.

ex13-002.text: One whole week, no yeast: seven days for the seven-day feast.

ex13-003.text: Shortest road, wrong road. God skipped Philistine country because war might send Israel running back to Egypt.

ex13-004.text: DAY = cloud canopy. When the sun is up, think shade.

ex13-005.text: NIGHT = fire torch. No sunlight, so God lights the road Himself.

ex13-006.text: Picture Moses leaving Egypt with a coffin. Not treasure—Joseph's bones.

ex13-007.text: Joseph packed for Canaan centuries early. He made Israel swear to carry up his bones when God brought them out.

ex14-001.text: See the trap: sea in front, Migdol beside them, Baal Zephon across from them.

ex14-002.text: MIGdol sounds like BIG wall. The fortified place is Migdol.

ex14-003.text: Pharaoh appears, dust flies, and courage disappears. Confident? Nope.

ex14-004.text: When the Lord fights, mouths close: hold your peace.

ex14-005.text: Big S for SOUTH splits the sea. Picture an S-shaped gust shoving the water apart.

ex14-006.text: Same sea, opposite ending: Israel walks through, Egypt does not.

ex14-007.text: See chariots and horsemen vanish when the water walls slam shut.

ex15-001.text: Horse and rider? Into the SEA. Hear the splash on the last word.

ex15-002.text: He saved me, so I glorify Him. Salvation leads to glorify.

ex15-003.text: Picture a divine name tag: “The LORD.” The blank is name.

ex15-004.text: Miriam is not just musical; she is Miriam the Prophetess.

ex15-005.text: Dance line, drums in hand: Miriam leads with timbrels.

ex15-006.text: They reach Marah, take a sip, and make a bitter face. Sweet water? False.

ex15-007.text: Marah's wood is a classic Cross-hook: wood hits bitter water, and bitterness turns sweet. Remember the answer by the object Moses throws in—a tree.

ex15-008.text: God posts His name at the healing place: “the Lord who heals you.” Marah becomes God’s clinic sign.

ex15-009.text: Elim is oasis math: 12 springs bubbling in the desert.

ex15-010.text: Seventy palms = a whole grove of 7-0 trees waving over the camp.

ex15-011.text: Picture a green island in the waste: twelve springs gushing and seventy palms swaying for shade, a resting place after the bitterness of Marah. The springs you drink (twelve) and the palms that shelter you (seventy) — fix seventy to the trees.
Lock the pair together: 12 springs, 70 palms.

ex16-003.text: ELIM to SIN: the oasis is behind them, and the manna wilderness is ahead. Think, “Leave Elim, enter Sin, then breakfast falls from heaven.”

ex16-004.text: They sigh for the 'flesh-pots' — the very cauldrons of their slavery — saying they'd rather have died full as slaves than walk free and hungry. This is Egypt's deepest chain: not the whip but the memory of its meat, the belly that begs to go back. The soul half-freed still craves the savory pots of its old bondage.
ex16-004.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

ex16-004.text: Picture Israel daydreaming about a steaming stew pot stamped EGYPT. The answer is the flesh pots, not rivers, kilns, or wells.

ex16-005.text: Chapter 15 sings; chapter 16 complains. Tambourines one moment, tummy-grumbling the next.

ex16-008.text: Breakfast falls from the sky, and everyone blurts, “What is it?” That question is the answer: manna.

ex16-009.text: Secret stash = stink and worms by sunrise. Manna was not pantry food; it had to be gathered fresh.

ex16-010.text: Day SIX, scoop TWICE. Double on the sixth so the seventh stays Sabbath-quiet.

ex16-011.text: Imagine the grumbling bouncing off Moses and Aaron like arrows, then landing on God. Exodus 16:8 says the complaint was really against the Lord.

ex16-012.text: Flesh in the EVENING, bread in the MORNING — evening meat, morning bread. The verse pairs them precisely so Israel knows both came from the Lord, not from luck or the land.

ex16-013.text: One person, one measured portion—each tent got its set daily ration. Think “one serving per eater,” not a free-for-all scoop.

ex16-014.text: Honey-wafer from heaven—basically a desert sky-cookie. If it sounds sweet, pick the wafer with honey.

ex16-017.text: The manna runs the full forty years, ceasing only at Canaan's border when the land could feed them. Paul calls it 'the same spiritual food,' a type fulfilled in Christ the Bread of Life who sustains the new Israel on her own pilgrimage, the provision lasting exactly as long as the journey home.
Forty years, forty breakfasts. The manna kept coming until Israel hit Canaan’s border.

ex16-018.text: Keep the desert miracles in separate boxes: chapter 16 rains bread and quail; chapter 17 spills water from a rock. So the NOT answer is the struck rock.
